title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for an ocean plastic cleanup expedition. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Turning the Tide: Ocean Cleanup Volunteers

texts.A:
### Sam, The Remote Island Cleanup
'We were miles from anywhere, surrounded by plastic,' Sam says. [51]The biggest challenge was the physical exhaustion of hauling heavy nets filled with debris over rocky terrain.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my chronic shoulder pain disappeared as my muscles grew stronger and more flexible.[/48] [53]I learned that the problem isn't just about what we throw away, but about the design of our entire global economy.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in data analysis was vital for documenting the types of plastic we found for research.[/50] [47]Initially, the total absence of digital connectivity was a shock, but I found it incredibly liberating.[/47] It was a sobering experience.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Coastal Scuba Expedition
Elena spent two weeks diving for 'ghost nets'. 'It was silent and tragic,' she recalls. [47]I hadn't expected the psychological toll of seeing trapped marine life to affect my sleep so much.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the expertise of the marine biologists, who turned every dive into a lesson in ecology.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of accomplishment that comes from freeing a single turtle from a net.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that our connection to the sea is far more profound than most city-dwellers realize.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a documentary on the subject, I’d never have known how to get involved.[/56] It was a life-changing mission.
texts.C:
### Marcus, The Urban River Project
Marcus focused on the source of the problem. 'We caught the plastic before it reached the sea,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the public's indifference, as people would often throw trash in the water while we were cleaning it.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general fitness and energy levels skyrocketed, which was a great personal side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that environmentalism isn't about grand gestures, but about the relentless daily work of many people.[/53] [50]I found that the organizational skills from my career as a event planner were perfect for managing the volunteer shifts.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the smell and the dirt, which I found quite repulsive at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried it would be too depressing, but the progress was visible.
texts.D:
### Sophie, The Arctic Beach Survey
'The plastic reaches even the most remote corners of the earth,' Sophie says. [49]The project made me realize that my personal lifestyle choices have consequences thousands of miles away.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have known about the expedition if my university hadn't offered it as a research internship.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie among the international team, who supported each other through the freezing weather.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the extreme cold to be such a constant drain on my physical and mental energy.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of sorting through tiny microplastics incredibly tedious and frustrating.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be tough enough for the conditions, but I proved myself wrong.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological toll of the expedition's environment?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) mentions the toll on her sleep, and Sophie (D) mentions the drain of the extreme cold.

questions.question: notes an unexpected physical benefit that occurred during the cleanup work?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) found his shoulder pain disappeared, and Marcus (C) saw his fitness levels skyrocket.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on the scale or connectivity of the plastic problem?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) reflects on the connection to the sea, and Sophie (D) on how personal choices affect remote areas.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the mission?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) used data analysis for research, and Marcus (C) used event planning for volunteer shifts.

questions.question: describes a technical or social challenge that made the work more difficult?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the exhaustion of hauling nets, and Marcus (C) the indifference of the public.

questions.question: admits to having had prior fears or having a strong emotional reaction to their progress?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) felt a sense of accomplishment freeing a turtle, and Marcus (C) worried it would be too depressing.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the global economy or environmentalism?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) reflects on the global economy's design, and Marcus (C) on the importance of daily work.

questions.question: highlights the value of the expertise or the support of the team?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) valued the expertise of the biologists, and Sophie (D) the camaraderie of the international team.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the less pleasant aspects of the task?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) adjusted to the smell/dirt, and Sophie (D) found sorting microplastics tedious.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external trigger or opportunity that led them to the project?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) saw a documentary, and Sophie (D) found it through a university internship.
